sections.heading: Do Atlas cluster's public IPs ever change?
sections.body:
The factors that determine if and when an Atlas cluster's public IP
address changes depends on the cluster tier.

> **Note:** This section applies to `M10` or larger dedicated clusters only
> unless specified.

An Atlas cluster's public IP addresses must change when you:

- [Scale your Replica Set to a Sharded Cluster](scale-cluster-sharding).

- [increase the number of shards](scale-cluster-shardNum).

- [Change the region into which a cluster is deployed](scale-cluster-region).

- [Required Access](scale-cluster-open-dialog) a Free cluster (formerly known as `M0`) or Flex cluster
  to an `M10` or larger cluster.

- [Terminate active clusters](terminate-cluster) then re-deploy a
  cluster with the same name *but in a different tier* within
  36 hours.

- [Encryption at Rest](scale-cluster-enable-encryption) on a cluster with a
  lifetime less than 12 hours.

> **Note:** For NVMe-backed clusters on [Amazon Web Services (AWS)](amazon-aws) or [Microsoft Azure](microsoft-azure),
> vertically scaling usually keeps the existing public IP addresses.
> However, there is a small chance they change; Atlas does not
> guarantee IP address retention.

An Atlas cluster's public IPs don't change when you:

- Vertically scale a cluster on [Amazon Web Services (AWS)](amazon-aws) or [Microsoft Azure](microsoft-azure) that is not
  backed by an [NVMe storage volumes](nvme-storage), or vertically
  scale a cluster from any tier on [Google Cloud Platform (GCP)](google-gcp).

- Unpause the cluster.

- [Terminate active clusters](terminate-cluster) and then re-deploy a cluster
  with a lifetime of 12 hours or more within 12-36 hours.

- Experience a maintenance or a healing event on your cluster.

To find the public IP address for any node in your cluster, use the
`nslookup` tool from the command line. The IP address is shown in the
`Address` portion of the output.

```sh
$ nslookup ds-shard-00-00-17jcm.mongodb-dev.net

Address: 34.226.104.79
```

sections.heading: Can I specify my own VPC for my MongoDB Atlas project?
sections.body:
No. An Atlas project, and its clusters, are associated with a
region-specific VPC (Virtual Private Cloud).

Atlas creates a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) when you deploy the first `M10+` dedicated
paid cluster to a given provider and region. For multi-region clusters,
Atlas creates one VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) per region if there is not already a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)
for that region.

*(AWS deployments only)* Atlas also creates a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) when you create
a VPC peering connection to an AWS (Amazon Web Services)
VPC (Virtual Private Cloud). Atlas creates the VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) in the same region as the peered
VPC (Virtual Private Cloud).

To use a different VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) (that is, on the customer's own cloud
infrastructure accounts), you would need to use MongoDB Cloud Manager
or Ops Manager.

sections.heading: How do I find my Atlas-side hostnames to open up my outbound firewall?
sections.body:
If your firewall blocks outbound network connections, you must configure
outbound access from your application environment to MongoDB Atlas.

sections.heading: Automatically Resolve IP Addresses
sections.body:
You can use the [SRV Connection Format](manual/reference/connection-string-formats),
which automatically resolves to the appropriate IP addresses for
MongoDB Atlas services and clusters.

sections.heading: Retrieve Specific IP Addresses for Outbound Firewall Rules
sections.body:
If your application environment requires configuring outbound firewall rules
using specific IP addresses, you can retrieve a list of IP addresses associated
with all clusters and services in your project using the
[Return All IP Addresses for One Project](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getgroupipaddresses)
API endpoint.

> **Note:** These IP addresses are dynamic and subject to frequent changes.
> Use this option only if your application can programmatically
> integrate these IP addresses into your orchestration automation,
> enabling frequent updates to your firewall rules.

sections.heading: How many cross-region network permissions does Atlas support?
sections.body:
Clusters can span regions and cloud service providers. The total number
of nodes in clusters spanning across regions has a specific constraint
on a per-project basis.

Atlas limits the total number of nodes in other regions in one
project to a total of 40, **not** including:

- Google Cloud regions communicating with each other

- Free clusters

- Flex clusters

Sharded clusters include additional nodes. The electable nodes on
the dedicated Config Server Replica Set (CSRS) count towards the
total number of allowable nodes. Each sharded cluster has an
additional electable node per region as part of the dedicated CSRS. To
learn more, see [Replica Set Config Servers](manual/core/sharded-cluster-config-servers).

The total number of nodes between any two regions must meet this
constraint.

> **Example:** If an Atlas project has nodes in clusters spread across three
> regions:
>
> - 30 nodes in **Region A**
>
> - 10 nodes in **Region B**
>
> - 5 nodes in **Region C**
>
> You can only add 5 more nodes to **Region C** because:
>
> - If you exclude Region C, Region A + Region B = 40.
>
>
> - If you exclude Region B, Region A + Region C = 35, \<= 40.
>
>
> - If you exclude Region A, Region B + Region C = 15, \<= 40.
>
>
> - Each combination of regions with the added 5 nodes still meets
>   the per-project constraint:
>
>   - Region A + B = 40 
>
>   - Region A + C = 40 
>
>   - Region B + C = 20 

You can't create a multi-region cluster in a project if it has one or
more clusters spanning 40 or more nodes in other regions.

Contact Atlas [support](manual/support) for questions
or assistance with raising this limit.

If you would exceed the cross-region permissions limit when creating a
cluster through the Atlas Administration API, the API (Application Programming Interface) returns the following
error:

```json
{
  "error" : 403,
  "detail" : "Cannot have more than 40 cross-region network permissions.",
  "reason" : "Forbidden"
}
```

sections.heading: Can Atlas be used with AWS Transit Gateway?
sections.body:
Yes. AWS (Amazon Web Services) PrivateLink powers Atlas Private Endpoints. This allows
for transitive connectivity. You can use the
[AWS Transit Gateway](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/tgw/tgw-getting-started.html)
with your VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) if you connected your VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) to Atlas via AWS (Amazon Web Services)
PrivateLink.

sections.heading: Can Atlas be used with AWS Direct Connect?
sections.body:
Yes. AWS (Amazon Web Services) PrivateLink powers Atlas Private Endpoints. This allows
for transitive connectivity. You can use [AWS Direct Connect](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/aws-vpc-connectivity-options/aws-direct-connect-network-to-amazon.html) with your
VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) if you connected your VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) to Atlas via AWS (Amazon Web Services) PrivateLink.
